name: Animation

unions.name: Animation
unions.comment: An animation describes how a value changes over a finite period of time.

values.name: andMap
values.comment:
Applies functions produced by one animation to values produced by another
one. In tandem with [`map`](#map), this allows you to combine several animations
using a multiple-parameter function.

    type alias Position =
        { x : Float, y : Float }

    position : Animation Position
    position =
        Position `Animation.map` x `Animation.andMap` y

    y : Animation Float
    y =
        Animation.interval pi
        |> Animation.map cos

    x : Animation Float
    x = Animation.interval pi
values.type: Animation (a -> b) -> Animation a -> Animation b

values.name: animate
values.comment:
Like [`run`](#run), but it also includes the amount of time *not* used up by
the animation.
values.type: Time -> Animation a -> ( Animation a, Time )

values.name: append
values.type: Animation a -> Animation a -> Animation a

values.name: continue
values.comment:
Glues on a new period of time to an animation.

    2
        |> Animation.immediately
        |> Animation.continue (2 * Time.second) (\x t -> x + 2 * t)
values.type: Time -> (a -> Float -> a) -> Animation a -> Animation a

values.name: immediately
values.comment: Creates an animation that takes no time and assumes the specified value.

values.name: interval
values.comment:
Runs linearily from 0 to 1 over the time specified.

For negative values you'll get the zero interval.

values.name: isDone
values.comment: True when the animation is over.

values.name: map
values.comment:
Builds an animation with the values transformed by a function. If you want
to use a function with multiple arguments, you're looking for
[`andMap`](#andMap)

values.name: reset
values.comment:
Moves the animation back to it's starting point - it's state before it
consumed any time.

values.name: reverse
values.comment: Reverses the arrow of time in the animation.

values.name: run
values.comment:
Runs an animation for some time. If you need to know when the animation
"ends early", leaving time to spare, consider using [`animate`](#animate)
instead.

values.name: sample
values.comment: Gets the current value of the animation.

values.name: time
values.comment:
Runs linearily from 0 to the time specified over the time specified.

For negative values you'll get the zero interval.

values.name: timeLeft
values.comment: The time left until the animation ends.
